model_output_raw_text: {"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 64","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"This run is fixed at 256 optimizer steps, so the current batch size only exposes the model to a small fraction of the training set before evaluation. Increasing batch size to 256 should substantially raise data coverage within the same step budget while staying simple and low risk for this CPU MLP workload.","secondary_modes":["summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 1e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"After increasing batch size, the optimizer is seeing much more data per step, so the current learning rate is likely conservative for this fixed-step regime. Doubling the LR is a simple next move that should improve convergence speed within 256 steps without changing the training recipe.","secondary_modes":["micro","summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 12","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"With the optimizer and step budget now working better, the next likely bottleneck is model capacity. A modest width increase from the current hidden sizes should give the MLP more representational headroom without changing the overall architecture or adding much complexity.","secondary_modes":["topk","micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"indexing","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"OPTIMIZER = \"adamw\"","old":"OPTIMIZER = \"adam\"","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""},{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 5e-4","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 1e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"indexing","rationale":"The current recipe is now training quickly enough that optimizer details are a likely next bottleneck. Switching from Adam to AdamW and modestly increasing decay is a simple way to keep the higher learning rate and larger batch while improving parameter updates and generalization.","secondary_modes":["caching","topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DEPTH = 3","old":"DEPTH = 2","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The current setup has improved optimization and batch coverage, so adding one more hidden block is a reasonable next capacity increase. This keeps the same MLP pattern and hyperparameters while testing whether extra depth helps the model use the larger effective training budget better than further optimizer tweaks.","secondary_modes":["indexing","micro"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 512","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 256","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The fixed 256-step budget still makes data coverage a first-order constraint, and this MLP remains lightweight enough that a larger batch is plausible on CPU. Moving to 512 should expose the model to substantially more examples within the same optimizer-step budget while preserving the current training dynamics.","secondary_modes":["topk","layout"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 3e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The jump to batch size 512 materially changed the optimization scale and produced a large gain, which suggests the current learning rate may again be conservative. A modest increase to 3e-3 is a simple follow-up that could convert the larger-batch stability into faster progress within the same 256-step budget.","secondary_modes":["micro","summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 3e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"The larger learning-rate probe degraded validation loss, so the simplest corrective move is to back out that change and restore the last clearly stronger optimization scale. This keeps the gains from the larger batch and deeper/wider MLP while removing the most recent destabilizing edit.","secondary_modes":["micro","summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 1024","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 512","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The strongest gains so far came from increasing data throughput per optimizer step, and the model is still small enough that another batch-size increase is a plausible next lever. Testing 1024 directly probes whether even more coverage within the fixed 256-step budget outweighs the reduced number of parameter updates per epoch-equivalent.","secondary_modes":["topk","layout"]}

{"declared_mode":"micro","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BATCH_SIZE = 2048","old":"BATCH_SIZE = 1024","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"micro","rationale":"The dominant improvement pattern has been higher data throughput per optimizer step, and the current model remains a plain CPU MLP with manageable activation sizes. Pushing batch size to 2048 is the clearest next test of whether this fixed-step workload is still coverage-limited rather than optimization-limited.","secondary_modes":["topk","layout"]}

{"declared_mode":"topk","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"LEARNING_RATE = 3e-3","old":"LEARNING_RATE = 2e-3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"topk","rationale":"At batch size 2048 the optimization scale has changed substantially again, and the current learning rate may now be conservative relative to the much smoother gradients. A modest increase to 3e-3 is a targeted way to test whether the model can convert the larger effective-epoch coverage into faster loss reduction without another large runtime jump.","secondary_modes":["micro","summaries"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 20","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 16","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The batch-size and learning-rate scaling have pushed optimization much further, so model capacity is a reasonable next bottleneck. A modest width increase should give the MLP more representational room while keeping the current successful training recipe intact and avoiding a larger structural rewrite.","secondary_modes":["micro","topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"DEPTH = 4","old":"DEPTH = 3","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The model is still improving from straightforward capacity increases, which suggests it has not saturated yet under the current large-batch regime. Adding one more hidden block is the cleanest next architecture probe to test whether extra depth can extract more value from the now much stronger optimization setup.","secondary_modes":["micro","topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 24","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 20","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The best recent changes have continued to come from modest capacity increases on top of the large-batch regime, suggesting the model still has headroom. Raising the base width one more notch is a simple extension of the current winning trend without introducing a new training dynamic.","secondary_modes":["micro","topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"layout","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"BASE_CHANNELS = 20","old":"BASE_CHANNELS = 24","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"layout","rationale":"The last width increase slightly worsened validation loss, so the cleanest next step is to revert to the previous base width that produced the current best result. This preserves the stronger large-batch, deeper configuration while removing the most recent over-capacity change.","secondary_modes":["micro","topk"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 2e-4","old":"WEIGHT_DECAY = 5e-4","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"The current model has already benefited from larger batch size, higher learning rate, and added capacity, so the remaining bottleneck may be slightly excessive regularization. Reducing AdamW weight decay is a simple, low-risk probe that can let the deeper, wider MLP fit more effectively within the fixed 256-step budget.","secondary_modes":["indexing","layout"]}

{"declared_mode":"caching","edit_format":"structured_edits","edits":[{"anchor":"","function":"","new":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss(label_smoothing=0.1)","old":"    criterion = nn.CrossEntropyLoss()","op":"replace_exact","source":"","text":""}],"primary_mode":"caching","rationale":"At this stage the run is effectively doing many passes over the data with a high-capacity MLP, so a small amount of output-side regularization is a sensible last probe. Label smoothing is simple, keeps the training loop unchanged, and can improve generalization without adding runtime or architectural complexity.","secondary_modes":["layout","indexing"]}
